{"ref_id": "corporate_acquisition_08_ep_006", "text": "DOCUMENT 1 OF 3
Type: Board Meeting Minutes
Date: January 21, 2025
Prepared by: Corporate Secretary Maria Fuentes
Distribution: Board of Directors
Classification: Board Confidential — Project Lighthouse

NEXTERA SOLUTIONS, INC.
MINUTES OF SPECIAL MEETING OF THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS
January 21, 2025

A special meeting of the Board of Directors of Nextera Solutions, Inc. was held on Tuesday, January 21, 2025, via secure video conference. The meeting commenced at 2:00 PM Pacific Standard Time.

DIRECTORS PRESENT:
- Robert Kessler, Board Chair
- Sarah Jiang, Board Member
- Tom Whitfield, Board Member
- Nina Patel, Board Member
- David Aldric, CEO (ex officio)

ALSO PRESENT:
- Maria Fuentes, Corporate Secretary

Board convened special session with all five members present. The Corporate Secretary confirmed a quorum was present and the meeting was called to order by Mr. Kessler at 2:02 PM PST. The meeting was held via secure video conference, not at HQ, at the direction of the Board Chair. No external advisors were present at this session. Total meeting duration: one hour and forty-eight minutes.

AGENDA ITEM 1 — CALL TO ORDER AND APPROVAL OF AGENDA

Mr. Kessler called the meeting to order and presented the agenda for approval. Mr. Whitfield moved to approve the agenda as distributed. Ms. Patel seconded. The agenda was approved unanimously. The Corporate Secretary noted that a revised agenda had been distributed the prior evening reflecting the addition of a new item under agenda item 7, labeled Strategic Exploration.

AGENDA ITEM 2 — MINUTES OF PRIOR MEETING

The minutes of the regular December 2024 Board meeting, previously distributed to all directors on January 14, were presented for approval. Ms. Jiang moved to approve the minutes as written. Mr. Whitfield seconded. The minutes were approved unanimously without amendment. The Corporate Secretary confirmed that executed copies had been filed in the company's corporate records.

AGENDA ITEM 3 — Q1 FORECAST REVIEW AND STANDARD FINANCIAL UPDATE

Kessler opened with the standard financial update and Q1 forecast review. The CFO had prepared written materials in advance, which had been distributed to all directors on January 17 via the board portal. Mr. Kessler summarized the key points from the financial package: revenue tracking above plan across all customer segments, the bookings pipeline showing strength entering the new fiscal quarter with several large enterprise deals expected to close in February, and operating expenses within budget for the quarter. Cash reserves remained healthy.

The Board reviewed the materials at length. Ms. Patel asked several clarifying questions regarding the timing of certain large enterprise deal closings expected in Q1, specifically the pipeline concentration in two accounts that together represented approximately eighteen percent of the quarter's bookings target. Mr. Kessler acknowledged the concentration risk and noted that the CFO had provided a sensitivity analysis in the appendix materials.

Mr. Whitfield asked about headcount plan adherence. The materials showed that the company had ended Q4 with four hundred and six employees, slightly below the planned headcount of four hundred and twelve. Mr. Aldric noted that the remaining open positions were primarily in engineering and that recruiting was on track to fill them by the end of February.

Ms. Jiang asked about gross margin trends. The Q1 forecast showed gross margins holding steady at approximately seventy-eight percent, consistent with prior quarters. No action was required on this agenda item.

AGENDA ITEM 4 — PRODUCT ROADMAP UPDATE

Aldric presented the product roadmap update including Axion Labs evaluation status. The presentation covered three major workstreams for Q1 2025. First, the recommendation engine version 3 launch, which was tracking toward a February production deployment after completing integration testing. Second, the Kubernetes infrastructure migration, which the engineering team had been preparing since Q4. Third, the ongoing evaluation of Axion Labs as a potential data partnership candidate.

On the recommendation engine, Mr. Aldric noted that all integration tests were passing and performance benchmarks were scheduled to begin the following day. He described this as the most significant product upgrade in the past eighteen months and expected it to have a material positive impact on customer engagement metrics.

On the Kubernetes migration, Mr. Aldric summarized the load testing results: the platform had sustained three times peak traffic for two hours with maximum latency remaining within acceptable limits. The migration window was targeted for the first week of February.

On the Axion Labs evaluation, Mr. Aldric reported that the product team had completed approximately sixty percent of planned customer interviews, with early results showing strong demand for real-time logistics data. He noted that the evaluation was proceeding well and that a decision on the partnership was expected in the first week of February. The Board asked questions about the competitive implications and the resource commitment required for integration. Mr. Aldric estimated eight to twelve weeks of dedicated engineering time and described it as consistent with standard partnership integrations the company had undertaken in prior years.

Ms. Patel asked about the Axion Labs evaluation timeline and whether it could be accelerated. Mr. Aldric replied that the evaluation was already on an aggressive timeline. Ms. Jiang asked about the cost of the partnership. Mr. Aldric deferred to the product team's final analysis but noted the estimated range was between one hundred eighty thousand and two hundred fifty thousand dollars per year.

AGENDA ITEM 5 — COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE AND MARKET POSITIONING

Board discussed competitive landscape and market positioning at the request of Mr. Kessler. Mr. Aldric provided an overview of recent market developments in the supply chain analytics space. Two venture-backed competitors had raised significant rounds in Q4 2024: DataFreight had closed a Series C at eighty-five million dollars, and ChainVue had raised a Series B at forty million dollars. Additionally, a larger enterprise player, OracleSoft, had announced a supply chain analytics module that would be bundled with their existing ERP product.

The Board discussed the implications for Nextera's pricing strategy and market share trajectory at length. Mr. Whitfield asked whether any of the competitive developments changed the company's growth assumptions. Mr. Aldric said they did not, citing Nextera's customer retention rate of ninety-six percent and net revenue retention of one hundred and twelve percent as evidence that the company's competitive position remained strong. Ms. Patel concurred and noted that the Board had discussed similar competitive dynamics in prior quarters without material impact on company performance.

Mr. Kessler asked about the company's win rate in competitive evaluations. Mr. Aldric reported that the win rate against direct competitors had been approximately sixty-five percent in Q4, consistent with prior quarters. The win rate against the larger enterprise bundled solutions was approximately forty percent, though those deals typically had longer sales cycles and different buyer profiles. The Board discussed strategies for improving win rates in the enterprise segment.

AGENDA ITEM 6 — GOVERNANCE AND COMPLIANCE UPDATE

The Corporate Secretary provided a brief governance update. The annual directors and officers insurance renewal was being processed with the current carrier, Hartford Financial. The policy premium was increasing approximately seven percent year over year, consistent with broader market trends in the D&O insurance market. The current policy provided twenty-five million dollars in coverage with a fifty-thousand-dollar retention.

The Board's committee assignments for 2025 had been finalized and distributed. Ms. Patel would continue to chair the Audit Committee, Mr. Whitfield would continue to chair the Compensation Committee, and Ms. Jiang would chair the newly formed Technology Advisory Committee.

Ms. Fuentes also noted that the company's SOC 2 Type II audit was scheduled for March 3 through 21 and that the CISO's team had begun preparation work. The CISO had reported no material gaps in the preliminary readiness assessment. Mr. Kessler asked whether any issues were anticipated. Ms. Fuentes said the CISO had reported no material concerns, noting that the company had achieved a clean SOC 2 Type II report in the prior year and that no significant changes to the control environment had occurred since then.

The Corporate Secretary also reminded the Board that the annual Board effectiveness self-assessment was due by February 15. Questionnaires had been distributed via the board portal.

No action was required on this agenda item.

AGENDA ITEM 7 — STRATEGIC EXPLORATION: Mr. Aldric informed the Board of preliminary inbound interest from Meridian Corp regarding a potential strategic transaction. The Board discussed the matter.

Mr. Aldric provided background on Meridian Corp, describing it as an $8.2 billion enterprise software conglomerate listed on the New York Stock Exchange. Meridian Corp operates across four business segments: enterprise resource planning, customer relationship management, supply chain management, and business intelligence. They employ approximately twelve thousand people globally with headquarters in Boston, Massachusetts.

Mr. Aldric stated that the inbound interest had come through an introduction from a mutual contact and that the initial conversations had been preliminary in nature. He characterized the interest as exploratory and noted that no formal offer had been made, no term sheet had been exchanged, and no financial advisors had been retained by either party. The discussions to date had consisted of two phone calls between Mr. Aldric and a senior executive at Meridian.

Mr. Kessler asked about the nature of the interest. Mr. Aldric described it as a general expression of interest in a strategic transaction, without specifying whether Meridian was contemplating an acquisition, a partnership, a joint venture, or some other form of combination. Mr. Aldric recommended that the Board authorize him to continue exploratory discussions to better understand Meridian's intent and to assess whether further engagement would be in the best interests of the company and its stockholders.

Mr. Whitfield asked about Meridian's acquisition history. Mr. Aldric said Meridian had completed four acquisitions in the past three years, ranging from approximately one hundred fifty million to one point two billion dollars. Their most recent acquisition was in Q3 2024, when they acquired a business intelligence startup called DataPrism for approximately four hundred million dollars.

Ms. Patel asked whether Mr. Aldric had any preliminary view on valuation. Mr. Aldric said it was premature to discuss valuation given the early stage of conversations. Ms. Patel noted that the Board should establish guidelines for any further discussions to protect the company's interests and suggested that the company should retain independent legal counsel and a financial advisor before engaging in any substantive valuation discussions.

Mr. Kessler asked whether the company's existing outside counsel at Morrison & Partners had experience with transactions of this nature. The Corporate Secretary noted that Morrison & Partners had represented the company since incorporation but that their M&A practice was relatively small. Ms. Patel suggested that the company consider engaging a larger firm with dedicated M&A expertise if discussions progressed.

The Board debated the matter at length. The discussion under agenda item 7 lasted approximately thirty-five minutes.

Mr. Kessler expressed the view that it would be prudent to continue the conversations to understand the opportunity, characterizing it as a normal exercise of the Board's fiduciary responsibilities. Mr. Whitfield agreed, noting that the Board had a fiduciary duty to understand all options available to maximize stockholder value. He referenced Revlon duties and noted that while they were not currently in a sale process, the Board should be informed about the landscape.

Ms. Patel supported continuing the discussions but recommended that external legal counsel be retained before any substantive negotiations began. She also recommended that the Board establish a special committee if discussions progressed beyond the exploratory phase.

Ms. Jiang expressed opposition to continuing the discussions. Ms. Jiang: "I want my opposition recorded. I believe our best path is organic growth and I do not support exploring this."

Ms. Jiang stated that the company's growth trajectory was strong, that the team had executed well across every metric that mattered, and that exploring a transaction at this stage could be distracting and demoralizing for employees if word leaked. She noted that inbound interest from larger companies was a regular occurrence for successful growth-stage companies and that the Board should not feel obligated to engage with every expression of interest. She further noted that the company had received similar inbound interest on at least three prior occasions during her Board tenure and had declined to engage each time.

Mr. Whitfield responded that the prior instances had involved companies that were not strategic fits. He noted that Meridian's supply chain management division made this inquiry qualitatively different from prior inbound interest.

After further discussion, Mr. Kessler called for a vote on the following resolution:

RESOLVED: The Board authorizes the CEO to continue exploratory discussions regarding Project Lighthouse, with updates to be provided at each regular Board meeting. Vote: 4-1 (Jiang opposed).

Mr. Kessler noted that the codename "Project Lighthouse" would be used for all future Board communications regarding this matter. All materials related to Project Lighthouse would be marked Board Confidential and restricted to Board members, the CEO, and the General Counsel. No information regarding Project Lighthouse was to be shared with any other company employee, officer, or advisor without prior Board approval.

Ms. Fuentes confirmed that a separate secure folder had been created on the board portal for Project Lighthouse materials and that access had been restricted to the five directors and the CEO.

AGENDA ITEM 8 — OTHER BUSINESS

No other business was raised by any director. Mr. Kessler reminded the Board of the upcoming February regular meeting and noted that the agenda would include an update on Project Lighthouse as a standing item.

AGENDA ITEM 9 — ADJOURNMENT

There being no further business to come before the meeting, Mr. Kessler adjourned the meeting at 3:50 PM PST. The next update is scheduled for the February regular Board meeting.

Respectfully submitted,

Maria Fuentes
Corporate Secretary
Nextera Solutions, Inc.

Subject: RE: Timeline Discussion — Project Lighthouse

My team has been tracking Nextera's public metrics — the Gartner placement, the G2 reviews, the customer case studies — and the picture is consistent with what you're describing. The recommendation engine is indeed what caught our attention initially, and the v3 improvements have only strengthened our conviction.

On integration plans: let me be very direct, because I know this matters to you.

Engineering is the crown jewel. Every engineer gets an offer letter. We plan to keep the SF office as a Meridian Center of Excellence for supply chain AI.

Here's how we're thinking about it in more detail:

1. SF Office: We intend to maintain your San Francisco location as a standalone Meridian development center. The working name internally is "Meridian Center of Excellence for Supply Chain AI." We see this as the natural home for the continued development of the recommendation engine and the broader supply chain optimization platform. We would expect to maintain the headcount at current levels or above.

2. Engineering Leadership: We want Marcus Webb to run the SF center. We understand he has a change-of-control provision in his employment agreement, and we're prepared to offer him an enhanced package on top of that. We have a good track record with engineering acquisitions — when we acquired LogicPath in 2022, we retained 94% of their engineering team through the 18-month integration period.

3. Product Continuity: Hannah Choi's product team would continue to drive the Nextera product roadmap within the Meridian portfolio. The Axion Labs integration work you're doing is exactly the kind of partnership that Meridian would support and potentially accelerate through our existing data relationships.

4. Brand: For the first 12-18 months, we would maintain the Nextera brand for the supply chain optimization product. The gradual transition to Meridian branding would be managed carefully to minimize customer disruption.

5. Support Functions: Sales and customer success would be integrated into Meridian's existing go-to-market organization. This is the area where we see the most overlap and where some consolidation would occur over time. Amanda Briggs's customer success team has done excellent work — we've heard great things from shared customers.

6. G&A: Finance, HR, legal, and administrative functions would consolidate into Meridian's corporate structure. This is standard in acquisitions of this size and typically takes 6-9 months.

I recognize that items 5 and 6 involve potential headcount impacts for some employees. Our approach has consistently been to make competitive offers to individuals we want to retain and to provide generous transition packages for those who don't continue. In the LogicPath acquisition, every employee received either an offer or a separation package equal to 6 months' salary.

My diligence team is ready to begin as soon as the data room is available. Our financial advisor (Lazard) has already reviewed the public information and is prepared to move quickly once they have access.

Gerald

DOCUMENT 2 OF 3
TYPE: All-Hands Meeting Transcript
DATE: February 23, 2025
SPEAKER: David Aldric (CEO)
SUBJECT: Late February All-Hands — Q1 Progress
LOCATION: Main Auditorium, 14th Floor + Zoom for Remote
ATTENDEES: 355 in-person, 61 remote (416 total)
DURATION: 55 minutes (35 min presentation + 20 min Q&A)

[TRANSCRIPT BEGINS]

DAVID ALDRIC: Good afternoon everyone, and thank you for being here — whether that's in the auditorium or joining us remotely. I see 355 badges scanned in and 61 on Zoom, which means we have essentially full attendance. I appreciate that, especially on a Friday.

Let me start with the headline: Q1 is shaping up to be our best quarter in company history. Revenue up, customers happy, product execution flawless. This is what winning looks like.

I'm going to run through the key metrics and then turn it over to Marcus, Hannah, and Kevin for department updates. Then we'll do our usual Q&A. I've set aside 20 minutes for questions because I know there are topics on people's minds and I want to make sure we have time for them.

[slide change]

REVENUE AND BOOKINGS

Let me start with the numbers. Q1 bookings are tracking at $14.1M as of yesterday. Our Q1 quota is $36.8M, which puts us at 38% attainment at day 48 of the quarter. For those of you who've been here a few years, you know that Q1 is always back-loaded — we typically book 55-60% of the quarter's revenue in March. So at 38% on day 48, we are right on pace.

Kevin's sales team has the strongest pipeline we've ever had. Pipeline coverage for the remaining target is over 3x. I'm not going to jinx it, but I will say: we're in a very good position.

Expansion revenue is at $3.8M quarter-to-date against an $8.2M annual target. That's being driven by some significant upsells — the LogiFlow expansion, DB Schenker, and several mid-market accounts adding capacity.

On churn: we lost Pacific Freight this month. That's $145K ACV. It's painful any time we lose a customer, and Amanda's team did a thorough analysis of what happened there. Price was the primary factor — the competitor came in at a significantly lower price point. Q1 total churn is $290K, well below our $600K annual target cap. Our overall retention metrics remain strong.

[slide change]

PRODUCT UPDATE — MARCUS WEBB, VP ENGINEERING

MARCUS WEBB: Thanks, David. I'll try to be brief because I know you all want to get to Q&A.

Three things from engineering this quarter.

First, rec engine v3. Adoption continues to accelerate. As of this morning, 112 customers have activated rec engine v3. That's 51% of our customer base, up from 78 at the end of January. The feedback has been exceptional — it was the number one positive driver in our NPS survey, cited 38 times in customer comments. Platform stability has been rock-solid at 99.99% uptime for February.

Second, the Kubernetes migration. Lisa Chen's infrastructure team completed the disaster recovery Kubernetes migration ahead of schedule. All three AWS regions are now running K8s 1.29. The total migration took 26 days with zero customer impact. I want to call out Lisa and her team specifically — this was a complex migration involving live traffic rerouting across three regions and they executed it flawlessly.

Third, sprint velocity. Sprint 4 just wrapped and we completed 38 stories and 890 points with zero carryover. That's a 12% increase in velocity quarter-over-quarter and it's the most productive quarter in our history. Our open pull request count is down to 18 from 23 in January, which means we're not just writing more code — we're shipping more code.

I'm incredibly proud of this team. When I look at what 186 engineers are accomplishing, it gives me enormous confidence in our ability to execute on the roadmap.

DAVID ALDRIC: Thank you, Marcus. Hannah?

[slide change]

PRODUCT ROADMAP — HANNAH CHOI, VP PRODUCT

HANNAH CHOI: Thank you. I'll be quick. Two items.

First, we have the Axion Labs integration demo scheduled for this Friday, February 28. This is the dry run — three engineers from Ben Arujo's team will be running through the full integration in our staging environment. The internal demo for the broader team is March 3. If you want to attend the internal demo, there's a calendar invite going out this afternoon.

The Axion integration is one of the most exciting partnerships we've done. For those who haven't been following closely, Axion Labs provides real-time freight market data — pricing, capacity, transit times — for 200+ shipping lanes globally. Integrating their data into our platform gives our customers a unified view of their supply chain with market intelligence built in. The beta is targeting June, with general availability in September.

Second, the Q2 roadmap prioritization is complete. I shared the detailed document on Confluence yesterday, but the highlights are: (1) Axion integration GA, (2) rec engine v4 planning, (3) advanced analytics dashboard, and (4) mobile app for field operations. The rec engine v4 planning is the biggest investment — we're looking at incorporating real-time market signals from the Axion data feed into the recommendation model, which would be a genuine first in the industry.

DAVID ALDRIC: Thank you, Hannah. Kevin?

[slide change]

SALES UPDATE — KEVIN OROSCO, VP SALES

KEVIN OROSCO: Afternoon, everyone. I'll keep this short.

Q1 bookings on track for our best quarter ever. The pipeline is the strongest I've seen in my three years here. We have 15 enterprise deals in late-stage negotiations totaling over $8M in potential ACV. The sales cycle is getting faster — average deal cycle time is 38 days this quarter, down from 42 days historically.

The market is moving in our direction. Supply chain optimization is not a nice-to-have anymore — it's a board-level priority for most logistics and manufacturing companies. Every RFP we see now has AI-driven optimization as a requirement, and that's where we win.

I also want to recognize the three new account executives who joined this month. Welcome to the team — you picked a great time to start.

[slide change]

DAVID ALDRIC: Thank you, Kevin.

CLOSING REMARKS

Let me close with something that I think is important for everyone to hear directly from me.

We are building for the next decade. Every decision I make is about ensuring Nextera's independence and long-term success. I know there's been speculation — let me be direct: we are not for sale.

I understand that in a market like this — where consolidation is happening across the enterprise software landscape — people naturally wonder about what's next for a company like ours. I've seen the Blind posts. I've heard the hallway conversations. And I want to address them head-on.

This company was founded nine years ago — our anniversary is March 28, and I'm told the events team has something special planned — with a vision of transforming how supply chains operate through intelligent automation. That vision has never been clearer or more achievable than it is right now. Our technology is the best in the market. Our customers are happier than they've ever been. Our financial performance is the strongest it's ever been. Why would I sell that?

The answer is: I wouldn't. And I'm not going to.

What I am going to do is keep investing in this business. We're hiring 20+ people in Q2. We just signed a lease for the 13th floor. We're building the Axion partnership. We're planning rec engine v4. These are not the actions of a company that's preparing to be acquired. These are the actions of a company that's building for the next decade.

So please: focus on the work. Focus on shipping great product. Focus on making customers successful. That's what got us here, and that's what will carry us forward.

Now let's do Q&A. I see some hands.

[Q&A SESSION — 20 minutes, 7 questions]

QUESTION 1 (remote, from Emily Park, Marketing):
Q: What's our plan for the industry conference in April? Are we doing a keynote this year?
A: Yes, we've been invited to deliver a keynote at SupplyChainExpo on April 15. Hannah and I will co-present on the Axion integration and the future of AI-driven supply chain optimization. It's a great stage for us.

QUESTION 2 (in-person, from Derek Nowak, Customer Success):
Q: Can you talk about the NPS results? Are we doing anything specific to address the detractor feedback?
A: Great question. NPS hit 64, up from 58. The primary improvement driver is rec engine v3 — our engineering team deserves enormous credit. On the detractor side, Amanda's team identified three accounts with pricing concerns and two with implementation speed concerns. We're doing a deep-dive on the red accounts on Monday. The pricing concerns are isolated to a few specific accounts and don't represent a systemic issue.

QUESTION 3 (in-person, from Jake Morrison, Engineering):
Q: Are there plans to expand the ML team beyond the two new hires? We're getting a lot of inbound interest in the rec engine and the current team is stretched.
A: Marcus?
MARCUS WEBB: We have two ML positions open now and we're looking at adding two more in Q2. The ML team is a priority — the rec engine work is driving measurable customer value and we want to invest ahead of demand. If you know great ML engineers, please refer them.

QUESTION 4 (in-person, from Samira Al-Rashid, Product):
Q: What's the timeline for the mobile app? We've been talking about it for two quarters.
A: Hannah?
HANNAH CHOI: It's on the Q2 roadmap as item number 4. We're targeting a design sprint in April with a working prototype by end of Q2. The initial scope is field operations — warehouse floor visibility and alert management. It won't be a full-featured mobile client for the platform, at least not in v1.

QUESTION 5 (remote, from Brian Teller, Sales):
Q: The retention bonus program mentions qualifying events including change of control. Should we read anything into that?
A: No. These are standard provisions that any well-run company includes. We added IPO as a qualifying event too — does that mean we're going public next week? Of course not.

The retention bonus program was designed to ensure that our key employees are rewarded for their loyalty and their contributions to the company's long-term success. It's a retention tool — that's literally in the name. The "qualifying events" language is standard legal drafting that covers the range of potential corporate events that could occur at any private company. We included change of control, IPO, and asset sales because those are the standard categories. I wouldn't read anything into the specific categories any more than I'd read into the fact that our fire insurance policy covers earthquakes — it doesn't mean we're expecting an earthquake.

Vanessa Kline's team can answer any specific questions you have about your individual agreement. The final enrollment deadline is March 15 and there's an information session on March 5.

QUESTION 6 (in-person, from Lisa Chen, Engineering):
Q: What's the status of the No Meeting Wednesdays experiment? Is it becoming permanent?
A: Vanessa?
VANESSA KLINE (from audience): We're collecting data through the engagement survey that launches March 3. Early feedback is positive — 89% of employees say they use Wednesdays for focused work. We'll make a permanent decision after the survey results are in, but I'd be surprised if we rolled it back given the response.

QUESTION 7 (in-person, from Tomas Lindgren, Customer Success):
Q: Can we get more detail on the Axion integration at the internal demo on March 3? Specifically, I have customers asking about data sovereignty — where does the Axion data live?
A: Hannah?
HANNAH CHOI: Absolutely. The March 3 demo will cover the architecture in detail. Short answer: Axion data is processed in our existing AWS infrastructure — same regions, same encryption, same compliance certifications. We're not sending customer data to Axion's systems. We're pulling market data from Axion into our platform. So from a data sovereignty perspective, it's a data ingestion model, not a data sharing model. Your customers' data stays exactly where it is.

DAVID ALDRIC: Great questions, everyone. That's all the time we have today. Let's finish Q1 strong. Thank you.

[TRANSCRIPT ENDS]

Post-meeting note: Employee sentiment poll conducted at conclusion of all-hands. Results: 58% "very confident" in company direction, 31% "somewhat confident," 8% "neutral," 3% "not confident." The "very confident" percentage is down from 61% at the previous all-hands in early February.

Total meeting duration: 55 minutes (35 min presentation, 20 min Q&A). 7 questions asked. All-hands recording available on Confluence by end of business Monday.

{"ref_id": "corporate_acquisition_08_ep_007", "text": "DOCUMENT 1 OF 3
Type: Legal Memorandum
Date: January 24, 2025
From: Nathan Foley, Senior Corporate Counsel
To: Rachel Dominguez, General Counsel
Subject: Executive Employment Agreement — Change of Control Provisions Review
Classification: Attorney-Client Privileged

MEMORANDUM

TO:      Rachel Dominguez, General Counsel
FROM:    Nathan Foley, Senior Corporate Counsel
DATE:    January 24, 2025
RE:      Review of Change-of-Control Provisions in Executive Employment Agreements

I. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

Per your request, I have completed the review of change-of-control provisions across all 14 executive employment agreements. This memorandum summarizes the current state of the provisions, identifies areas where our agreements deviate from current market practice, and proposes revisions to align with benchmarking data from the Compensia 2024 Executive Compensation Survey. The proposed revisions differentiate between C-suite and VP-level executives, reflecting the market trend toward tiered acceleration structures.

II. SCOPE OF REVIEW

I reviewed executive employment agreements for the following individuals:

C-Suite Executives (5):
1. David Aldric, Chief Executive Officer — agreement effective March 15, 2019 (date of hire), last amended October 2023
2. Priya Vasquez, Chief Financial Officer — agreement effective June 1, 2020, last amended October 2023
3. Marcus Webb, Chief Technology Officer — agreement effective January 10, 2020, last amended October 2023
4. Rachel Dominguez, General Counsel — agreement effective September 1, 2021, last amended October 2023
5. Vanessa Kline, Vice President of People — agreement effective February 15, 2021, last amended October 2023

VP-Level Executives (9):
6. Hannah Choi, Vice President of Product — agreement effective April 1, 2022
7. Kevin Orosco, Vice President of Sales — agreement effective July 15, 2021
8. Amanda Briggs, Vice President of Customer Success — agreement effective November 1, 2021
9. Jordan Michaels, Vice President of Finance — agreement effective March 1, 2022
10. Frank Gutierrez, Chief Information Security Officer — agreement effective August 1, 2022
11. Lisa Chen, Director of Platform (VP-equivalent per agreement terms) — agreement effective May 15, 2020
12. Sami Okoro, Engineering Manager (VP-equivalent per agreement terms) — agreement effective January 3, 2022
13. Derek Hollis, FP&A Manager (VP-equivalent per recent promotion agreement) — agreement effective September 1, 2023
14. Angela Torres, Controller (VP-equivalent per agreement terms) — agreement effective March 15, 2021

Executive agreements reviewed: 14 total (5 C-suite, 9 VP-level).

III. CURRENT PROVISIONS

Current provisions: all executives receive double-trigger acceleration (termination + change of control). I recommend aligning with current market practice. The current change-of-control provisions are identical across all 14 agreements and contain the following terms:

A. Change of Control Definition

The current change-of-control definition in all 14 agreements is as follows: acquisition of 50% or more of voting shares or substantially all assets of the company by a person or group. The definition also includes certain board composition changes (a majority of the board being replaced within any 12-month period by directors whose appointment was not endorsed by a majority of the existing board).

The current definition does not address mergers where the company's stockholders retain less than 50% of the surviving entity, which is a common market provision. I recommend adding this to the revised definition. Additionally, the current definition does not address tender offers or reverse mergers, which are increasingly common transaction structures in the enterprise software sector. For completeness, I recommend including these in the revised definition as well.

I also note that the current definition does not include a carve-out for internal restructurings or reorganizations that do not involve a third-party acquirer. This is standard market practice and should be added to prevent inadvertent triggering of acceleration provisions in connection with routine corporate transactions such as holding company formations or divisional restructurings.

B. Acceleration Trigger

All 14 agreements currently contain a double-trigger acceleration mechanism: 100% of unvested equity accelerates only upon both (i) a change of control and (ii) the executive's involuntary termination without cause or resignation for good reason within 12 months following the change of control.

The "cause" definition in the current agreements includes: material breach of the agreement, conviction of a felony, willful misconduct causing material harm to the company, or failure to perform duties after written notice and 30-day cure period. The "good reason" definition includes: material reduction in base salary (more than 10%), material reduction in authority or responsibilities, relocation of more than 50 miles, or material breach of the agreement by the company.

C. Severance Terms

All 14 agreements currently provide 12 months of base salary as severance upon a qualifying double-trigger event. There is no distinction between C-suite and VP-level severance under the current agreements. The severance is paid in a lump sum within 30 days of the qualifying termination, subject to the executive's execution of a general release of claims. The agreements also provide for 12 months of continued COBRA benefits at the company's expense.

D. Non-Competition and Non-Solicitation

All 14 agreements contain 12-month post-termination non-competition and 18-month non-solicitation covenants. These covenants apply regardless of the circumstances of termination. I note that the enforceability of non-competition provisions varies by jurisdiction and that California, where 12 of 14 executives are based, generally does not enforce post-employment non-competition covenants under Business and Professions Code Section 16600. The non-solicitation provisions are generally enforceable in California, however.

E. Estimated Values

At the current 409A valuation of $14.20 per share, the estimated equity value subject to acceleration across all 14 executives is approximately $42 million. This figure breaks down as follows:

- CEO (Aldric): $12.4M in unvested equity (874,648 unvested shares)
- CFO (Vasquez): $6.8M in unvested equity (478,873 unvested shares)
- CTO (Webb): $5.2M in unvested equity (366,197 unvested shares)
- GC (Dominguez): $4.1M in unvested equity (288,732 unvested shares)
- VP People (Kline): $3.5M in unvested equity (246,479 unvested shares)
- VP-level executives (9 total): $10.0M in unvested equity aggregate (704,225 unvested shares)

Total shares subject to acceleration: 2,959,154

IV. MARKET BENCHMARKING

I reviewed the Compensia 2024 Executive Compensation Survey, which covers compensation practices at 287 technology companies with revenues between $50 million and $500 million. The survey, published in September 2024, is the most current source of market benchmarking data available for companies at our revenue stage. The survey reports the following market practices for change-of-control provisions:

Single-Trigger Acceleration (C-Suite):
- 68% of surveyed companies provide single-trigger acceleration for CEO
- 54% provide single-trigger acceleration for other C-suite executives
- Median acceleration: 100% of unvested equity
- Trend: The percentage of companies offering single-trigger acceleration to C-suite has increased from 58% in the 2022 survey to 68% in 2024, reflecting a clear market shift

Double-Trigger Acceleration (VP-Level):
- 82% of surveyed companies use double-trigger acceleration for VP-level executives
- Most common employment requirement: 12 months post-closing
- Median acceleration: 100% of unvested equity upon qualifying termination
- Only 11% of companies provide single-trigger acceleration at the VP level

Severance Multiples:
- CEO median: 24 months base salary + target bonus
- Other C-suite median: 18 months base salary + target bonus
- VP-level median: 12 months base salary (no bonus multiplier)
- 73% of companies provide COBRA continuation for the severance period

Change of Control Definition:
- 91% include acquisition of 50%+ voting shares
- 87% include sale of substantially all assets
- 79% include mergers resulting in stockholder dilution below 50%
- 62% include board composition changes

V. PROPOSED REVISIONS

Based on the benchmarking analysis, I propose the following revisions to align with current market standards:

A. C-Suite Executives (CEO, CFO, CTO, GC, VP People)

Proposed revision for C-Suite (CEO, CFO, CTO, GC, VP People): single-trigger acceleration of 100% of unvested equity upon a qualifying change-of-control event. This means that upon a change of control, all unvested equity would accelerate immediately without any requirement for termination. This change aligns with the majority practice (54-68%) reported in the Compensia survey.

Proposed severance for C-suite: 24 months base salary + target bonus. This is an increase from the current 12 months base salary and aligns with the median practice reported in the Compensia survey. The aggregate severance exposure for the 5 C-suite executives, based on current compensation, is approximately $5.8 million (salary component only) plus approximately $2.9 million in target bonus, for a total of approximately $8.7 million.

COBRA continuation: extended from 12 months to 24 months for C-suite executives, consistent with the severance period.

B. VP-Level Executives

VP-level executives: double-trigger acceleration with a 12-month post-closing continued employment requirement. This aligns with the 2024 Compensia survey data. Under this provision, VP-level executives would receive 100% acceleration of unvested equity only if they are terminated without cause or resign for good reason within 12 months following the change of control.

Proposed severance for VP-level: 12 months base salary. This maintains the current severance level for VP-level executives, which is at the market median. No change to the COBRA continuation period for VP-level executives.

C. Updated Change of Control Definition

I recommend expanding the change-of-control definition to include:
- Acquisition of 50% or more of voting shares
- Sale of substantially all assets
- Merger or consolidation where existing stockholders hold less than 50% of surviving entity
- Board composition change (majority replacement within 12 months)
- Tender offer resulting in acquirer holding more than 50% of outstanding shares
- Carve-out: excludes internal restructurings, recapitalizations, and IPO transactions

This expanded definition is consistent with market practice and provides clearer coverage for a broader range of transaction structures.

VI. IMPLEMENTATION

I recommend the following implementation approach:

1. Present the proposed revisions to the Compensation Committee for review — targeting February 2025
2. Obtain Board approval at the next regular Board meeting — targeting February 2025
3. Prepare amended employment agreements for execution — within 30 days of Board approval
4. Coordinate with VP People on communication to affected executives — concurrent with agreement execution
5. File updated equity plan provisions with the SEC as required — within 10 business days of amendment

The estimated timeline is:
- Compensation Committee review: first week of February 2025
- Board approval: February 2025 Board meeting (tentatively February 10)
- Agreement execution: within 30 days of Board approval
- Employee notification: concurrent with agreement execution

I recommend Board approval at the next regular meeting. The documentation required for Board review includes: (1) this memorandum, (2) redlined versions of each executive agreement showing proposed changes, (3) a summary of the Compensia benchmarking data, and (4) a financial impact analysis prepared by the CFO's office.

VII. TAX CONSIDERATIONS

I have consulted with our outside tax counsel at Morrison & Partners regarding the Section 280G implications of the proposed changes. The single-trigger acceleration for C-suite executives could result in parachute payments that exceed the safe harbor threshold under Section 280G. The safe harbor threshold is three times the executive's "base amount" (defined as the average W-2 compensation over the preceding five years).

I recommend that the revised agreements include a best-of-net provision, which would reduce payments to the safe harbor amount only if the executive would receive a greater after-tax benefit. This approach avoids the need for stockholder approval under Section 280G(b)(5)(B) and is the most common approach among our peer group (used by 64% of companies in the Compensia survey).

The estimated Section 280G exposure based on current compensation levels and equity values is approximately $8.2 million across all C-suite executives. This figure would change based on the actual transaction price in any future change-of-control event. Morrison & Partners has provided detailed calculations for each C-suite executive, which are available upon request.

I also note that the Section 409A implications of the proposed changes need to be reviewed. The acceleration of vesting upon a change of control is generally not treated as a modification of the equity award for 409A purposes if the acceleration is provided under the equity plan rather than as an individual arrangement. Morrison & Partners is reviewing this issue and will provide a supplemental opinion by January 31.

VIII. PEER COMPANY COMPARISON

For additional context, I compiled change-of-control provisions from publicly available information at five peer companies in the enterprise software space with comparable revenue and employee count:

1. Veeva Systems (FY2024 revenue: $2.4B) — Single-trigger for CEO, double-trigger for all others, 18-month post-closing period
2. Datadog (FY2024 revenue: $2.1B) — Single-trigger for C-suite, double-trigger for VP+, 12-month post-closing period
3. Confluent (FY2024 revenue: $780M) — Single-trigger for CEO and CFO only, double-trigger for all others
4. Braze (FY2024 revenue: $470M) — Double-trigger for all executives, 12-month post-closing period
5. nCino (FY2024 revenue: $480M) — Single-trigger for C-suite, double-trigger for VP+, 12-month post-closing period

Our proposed structure (single-trigger for C-suite, double-trigger for VP-level) is consistent with the most common approach among our peers.

IX. CONCLUSION

The proposed revisions bring our executive agreements in line with current market practice as reported in the Compensia 2024 survey. The key changes are: (1) moving from double-trigger to single-trigger acceleration for C-suite executives, (2) raising C-suite severance to 24 months base salary plus target bonus, (3) standardizing the change-of-control definition, and (4) maintaining double-trigger acceleration with 12-month employment requirement for VP-level executives. These changes would apply to all 14 executive agreements.

I am available to discuss these recommendations at your convenience and can prepare the draft amended agreements upon your approval. I anticipate the drafting process will take approximately one week once direction is given.

Respectfully submitted,

Nathan Foley
Senior Corporate Counsel
Nextera Solutions, Inc.

--- Original Message ---
From: Vanessa Kline <v.kline@nexterasolutions.com>
To: David Aldric <d.aldric@nexterasolutions.com>, Rachel Dominguez <r.dominguez@nexterasolutions.com>
Date: January 24, 2025, 9:08 AM PST
Subject: Equity Acceleration Policy — Proposed Updates

David, Rachel,

Per our discussion, I've drafted updates to the equity acceleration policy. The key change is adding a 'qualifying event' definition that would trigger acceleration for eligible employees.

I've been working on this since our conversation last week and have incorporated the feedback from our preliminary review. Here's a summary of the proposed changes and the rationale:

1. QUALIFYING EVENT DEFINITION

We're proposing to add a "qualifying event" definition to the Nextera Equity Plan. Currently, the equity plan does not contain a standalone definition of qualifying events that trigger acceleration. The acceleration provisions are embedded in individual employment agreements, which creates inconsistency across the employee population.

The proposed qualifying event definition includes:
- Change of control (acquisition of 50% or more of voting shares or substantially all assets)
- Initial public offering
- Specified asset sale exceeding $50 million

This definition would be incorporated into the equity plan itself, creating a single source of truth for all participants. Currently, 380 of our 406 employees hold equity under the plan, so this change affects the vast majority of our workforce.

I've reviewed the definitions used by comparable companies in the Compensia survey and our proposed language is consistent with the most common formulations. I've also ensured alignment with the language Nathan Foley is using in the executive agreement revisions.

2. ACCELERATION TIERS

The proposed policy creates three tiers of acceleration upon a qualifying event:

Tier 1 (C-Suite): Single-trigger acceleration of 100% of unvested equity
  - Applies to: CEO, CFO, CTO, GC, VP People (5 individuals)
  - Trigger: qualifying event alone, no termination required
  - Rationale: aligns with 68% of peer companies for CEO, 54% for other C-suite

Tier 2 (VP-Level): Double-trigger acceleration of 100% of unvested equity, requiring termination within 12 months post-closing
  - Applies to: 9 VP-level executives
  - Trigger: qualifying event + involuntary termination without cause or resignation for good reason
  - Rationale: aligns with 82% of peer companies at VP level

Tier 3 (All Other Employees): Double-trigger acceleration of 50% of unvested equity, requiring termination within 12 months post-closing
  - Applies to: 366 employees holding equity
  - Trigger: qualifying event + involuntary termination without cause
  - Rationale: new benefit not previously available; 50% acceleration is at the upper end of market practice for non-executive employees

This tiered approach is consistent with the recommendations in Nathan Foley's legal memo, which I understand he submitted to Rachel today.

3. FINANCIAL IMPACT

Total unvested equity pool: $68 million at the current 409A valuation of $14.20 per share.

Breakdown by tier:
- Tier 1 (C-Suite, 5 individuals): $32.0M in unvested equity
- Tier 2 (VP-Level, 9 individuals): $10.0M in unvested equity
- Tier 3 (All other employees, 366 individuals): $26.0M in unvested equity

The maximum acceleration exposure under the proposed policy (assuming all triggers are met for all tiers) is the full $68 million. In practice, Tier 2 and Tier 3 would only accelerate upon qualifying terminations. Based on industry data, approximately 30-40% of employees experience qualifying terminations within 12 months of a change of control, so the expected exposure is more likely in the range of $40-50 million.

For budgeting and board presentation purposes, I've prepared three scenarios:
- Conservative (Tier 1 only triggers): $32.0M
- Base case (Tier 1 + 35% of Tier 2/3): $44.6M
- Full exposure (all tiers, all triggers): $68.0M

I've also noted that the 409A valuation of $14.20 per share was set in October 2024 and is scheduled for an update in April 2025. Any qualifying event would likely result in a transaction price significantly above the 409A valuation, so the actual dollar exposure would exceed these estimates.

4. COMMUNICATION PLAN

I'm planning to communicate the updated policy as follows:
- Board approval: target the February Board meeting
- Company-wide announcement: within one week of Board approval, via all-hands presentation
- Employee information session: scheduled for February 10 (tentative), in-person + Zoom
- FAQ document: posted to HR intranet concurrent with announcement
- Individual equity statement updates: distributed within 30 days of effective date
- Manager toolkit: talking points and Q&A guide distributed to all managers 48 hours before company announcement

I'd like both of your input on the communication approach. The key message I want to convey is that this is a routine update to align with market best practices, not a response to any specific event. We want employees to see this as a positive enhancement to their compensation package.

5. RETENTION ANALYSIS

I've also analyzed the retention implications of the proposed changes. With 380 equity holders, the acceleration provisions are a meaningful retention tool. Here's the analysis:

Average unvested equity value by level:
- C-Suite: $6.4M per executive
- VP-Level: $1.1M per executive
- Senior IC/Manager: $142K per employee
- Mid-level: $68K per employee
- Junior: $22K per employee

The Tier 3 acceleration provision (50% acceleration on qualifying termination) represents a new benefit worth approximately $11K-$71K per employee depending on level. For senior ICs and managers, this is a material benefit that strengthens retention.

Our current voluntary turnover rate is 8.2% (trailing 12 months). Among employees in the top quartile of unvested equity value, voluntary turnover is 3.1%. The acceleration provision reinforces the retention value of unvested equity by providing protection against involuntary loss of equity in a change-of-control scenario.

6. TIMELINE

- January 24: Submit to legal for review (today)
- January 31: Receive legal redlines
- February Board meeting: Board approval
- March 1: Effective date

Please let me know if you have questions or want to discuss further.

Best,
Vanessa Kline
VP People
Nextera Solutions

2. Vesting schedules (we're at 4-year with 1-year cliff, which is standard)
   - 89% of companies in the Compensia survey use 4-year vesting
   - 74% use a 1-year cliff, which is what we do
   - Monthly vesting after cliff (our approach) is used by 68% of companies

3. Refresh grant cadence and size (we currently do annual refreshes at ~25% of initial grant)
   - Market median for annual refresh is 20-25% of initial grant
   - We're at the upper end of market practice, which is good for retention
   - Some companies are moving to semi-annual refreshes, but that's still a minority practice

4. Burn rate analysis (our annual equity burn rate is approximately 3.8%, which is within the ISS guideline of 5%)
   - ISS benchmark for technology companies: 5.0% burn rate threshold
   - Our 3.8% gives us headroom for additional grants without exceeding guidelines
   - Peer median: 4.1%

5. Overhang analysis (total dilution from outstanding and available equity)
   - Current overhang: 18.2% (shares outstanding + available pool as % of fully diluted shares)
   - Peer median: 19.5%
   - We're slightly below peer median, which is favorable

6. Peer comparison (I'll include the same 5 peers Nathan used in his memo, plus 2 additional)

I'll also include a section on ESPP — our current 15% discount is at market and the participation rate is 44% of eligible employees. I'll benchmark that as well.

One more thought: should I include a retention risk analysis in the board presentation? I can model the impact of different equity scenarios on retention for our top performers. We have the data from our last engagement survey that correlates equity satisfaction with retention intent.

Best,

{"ref_id": "corporate_acquisition_08_ep_016", "text": "DOCUMENT 1 OF 4
TYPE: Email Thread
DATE: February 20, 2025
FROM: Nathan Foley (Senior Corporate Counsel)
TO: Rachel Dominguez (General Counsel)
SUBJECT: Comprehensive Change-of-Control Summary — Draft Review
CLASSIFICATION: Attorney-Client Privileged

--- Original Message ---
From: Nathan Foley
To: Rachel Dominguez
Date: February 20, 2025, 9:14 AM PST
Subject: Comprehensive Change-of-Control Summary — Draft Review

Rachel,

Draft comprehensive CoC summary attached. 377 agreements reviewed. Key findings below.

I've completed the comprehensive review of every existing agreement in the company's contract management system for change-of-control provisions. The review spanned four major categories: employment agreements, vendor contracts, customer agreements, and real estate leases. The project required approximately three weeks of concentrated effort, including pulling agreements from both our current CLM platform and the archived legacy files that predated the system migration in 2022. I'm confident we have captured every material provision.

Below is the detailed breakdown by category with observations on each.

EMPLOYMENT AGREEMENTS

Of the 416 current employees, 14 executive-level employment agreements contain explicit change-of-control provisions. These 14 agreements follow the standard executive employment template that has been in use since the board approved the revised executive compensation framework in Q2 2021. Each agreement includes the following provisions triggered upon a qualifying change-of-control event:

(a) Accelerated vesting of all outstanding equity awards, including stock options and restricted stock units, with vesting occurring immediately prior to the closing of the change-of-control transaction;

(b) Severance multipliers ranging from 1.5x to 3x base salary depending on the executive's tier designation, payable in a lump sum within 30 days of termination following a change of control;

(c) COBRA continuation coverage for 18 to 24 months, depending on the executive's tier designation, with the company paying the full premium cost during the continuation period;

(d) A prorated annual bonus based on target bonus percentage and the number of days employed during the fiscal year in which the change of control occurs.

I've catalogued the specific trigger definitions for each of the 14 agreements. The definitions are substantially similar but not identical — the agreements executed in 2021 and 2022 use a broader definition that includes asset sales exceeding 50% of the company's total assets, while the agreements executed in 2023 and 2024 use a narrower definition that requires a change in majority ownership of voting shares. This discrepancy should be harmonized if the company enters into any transaction that could trigger the broader definition without triggering the narrower one.

The remaining 402 non-executive employee agreements do not contain individual change-of-control provisions. Those employees are covered under the company's general severance plan, which was recently restructured effective March 1, 2025, reducing non-executive severance from a 2x base salary multiplier to a 1x base salary multiplier. The general severance plan does not contain a change-of-control trigger — it applies only in cases of involuntary termination without cause.

A few notes on the employment category:

- Two of the 14 executive agreements (CFO Priya Vasquez and VP Engineering Marcus Webb) include additional "gross-up" provisions for excise taxes under Section 280G/4999 of the Internal Revenue Code. These were negotiated as part of their original offer packages and are not present in any other executive agreement.

- The General Counsel (Rachel Dominguez) agreement includes a specific provision requiring continuation of D&O insurance coverage for 6 years following a change of control, which is separate from the standard tail coverage in the company's D&O policy.

- VP People Vanessa Kline's agreement includes a provision requiring 90 days' advance notice of any contemplated reduction in force following a change of control, which is unique among the executive agreements.

VENDOR CONTRACTS

42 vendor contracts require notification within 30 days of a change-of-control event. 18 customer contracts require affirmative consent from the customer prior to assignment. 3 leases require landlord consent for assignment.

The vendor notification obligations are straightforward in the majority of cases. Most use boilerplate language requiring written notice within 30 calendar days of the effective date of any change-of-control event. The notification is informational only — none of these 42 contracts grant the vendor a termination right triggered solely by change of control. However, several contracts do include provisions that allow the vendor to renegotiate pricing or modify service terms following a change of control, which could have financial implications.

I've organized the vendor contracts by tier based on annual spend:

Tier 1 (annual spend exceeding $500,000 per year): 8 contracts
- Amazon Web Services — Enterprise Agreement ($4.2M annual, multi-year term expiring December 2026). CoC provision requires 30 days' written notice plus a certification that the successor entity will assume all obligations under the existing service terms. AWS retains the right to conduct a credit review of the successor entity and may require modified payment terms based on that review.
- Snowflake — Data Cloud Agreement ($1.8M annual, term expiring March 2026). Standard 30-day notification. Includes a most-favored-nation pricing clause that survives assignment.
- Salesforce — Enterprise License ($980K annual, 3-year term). Standard 30-day notification with automatic assignment to successor entity.
- Datadog — Monitoring Platform ($720K annual, annual renewal). Standard notification. Month-to-month cancellation provision unaffected by CoC.
- Cloudflare — Enterprise Security ($640K annual, 2-year term). Standard notification.
- Okta — Identity Management ($580K annual, 3-year term). Standard notification. Includes data processing addendum that requires separate counterparty acknowledgment.
- Confluent — Event Streaming Platform ($560K annual, annual). Standard notification.
- Elastic — Search and Observability ($510K annual, 2-year term). Standard notification.

Tier 2 (annual spend $100,000 to $500,000): 14 contracts across various SaaS and infrastructure providers including Twilio, SendGrid, PagerDuty, CircleCI, GitHub Enterprise, Jira/Atlassian, Figma, Notion, Zoom, Slack, and four others. All contain standard 30-day notification provisions without material variations.

Tier 3 (annual spend under $100,000): 20 contracts with primarily professional services firms, staffing agencies, and specialty consultants. All standard notification provisions.

Total annual vendor spend covered by CoC notification requirements: approximately $14.8M.

CUSTOMER CONTRACTS

18 customer contracts contain change-of-control consent requirements. These are enterprise customer agreements negotiated with counterparties who had sufficient leverage during the sales cycle to include assignment restrictions. Unlike the vendor notifications, these require affirmative written consent from the customer before any assignment or transfer of the agreement in connection with a change of control.

I've prepared a preliminary risk assessment for each of the 18 customer contracts, considering factors such as:
- Remaining contract term and renewal date
- Historical renewal rate and expansion history
- Current customer health score from the CS team's dashboard
- The customer's historical approach to consent requests in other vendor relationships
- Whether the customer has an active competitive evaluation underway
- The customer's internal procurement policies for consent approvals

The combined annual contract value of these 18 agreements is $8.4M. I'll have a more detailed financial breakdown in the highlighted list you've requested.

Key customer contracts requiring consent (top 5 by ACV):
1. GlobalShip Industries — $890K ACV, renewed January 2025, 3-year term, green health
2. DB Schenker — $420K ACV, expansion in Q1, 2-year term, green health
3. Maersk Digital — $380K ACV, stable, 2-year remaining, green health
4. Ceva Logistics — $310K ACV, stable, 18 months remaining, yellow health
5. TransGlobal Logistics — $180K ACV, recently evaluated competitors, red health

LEASE AGREEMENTS

3 office leases contain change-of-control assignment restrictions:

1. 14th Floor — Headquarters (Primary lease)
   - Landlord: Meridian Real Estate Holdings (no relation to Meridian Corp)
   - Lease area: 22,000 sq ft
   - Term: through December 2029
   - Monthly rent: $110,000 ($60/sq ft annual)
   - CoC provision: Requires landlord consent for assignment. Includes a recapture right allowing the landlord to terminate the lease and reclaim the space in lieu of consenting to assignment. Landlord has 30 days to exercise recapture right after receiving notice.

2. 13th Floor — Engineering Expansion (New lease)
   - Landlord: Meridian Real Estate Holdings
   - Lease area: 8,000 sq ft
   - Term: 18 months from March 1, 2025
   - Monthly rent: $48,000 ($72/sq ft annual, premium rate for short-term)
   - CoC provision: Requires landlord consent for assignment. No recapture right. Shorter term reduces landlord risk.

3. Warehouse Facility — Data Center Equipment
   - Landlord: Pacific Industrial Partners
   - Lease area: 3,200 sq ft
   - Term: through June 2026
   - Monthly rent: $8,800 ($33/sq ft annual)
   - CoC provision: Standard consent requirement. Landlord may not unreasonably withhold consent.

SUMMARY TABLE

| Category | Total Reviewed | CoC Provision | Notification Only | Consent Required |
|----------|---------------|---------------|-------------------|------------------|
| Employment | 416 | 14 | — | — |
| Vendor | 168 | 42 | 42 | 0 |
| Customer | 218 | 18 | 0 | 18 |
| Lease | 5 | 3 | 0 | 3 |
| **Total** | **377** | **77** | **42** | **21** |

Contracts requiring counterparty consent: 21 (18 customer + 3 lease). Contracts requiring notification only: 42 vendor.

I delivered this draft 2 days ahead of the February 28 deadline you originally set. Let me know if any revisions are needed before finalization.

Best,
Nathan Foley
Senior Corporate Counsel
Nextera Solutions

--- Reply ---
From: Rachel Dominguez
To: Nathan Foley
Date: February 20, 2025, 11:42 AM PST
Subject: RE: Comprehensive Change-of-Control Summary — Draft Review

Nathan,

Good work. Please prepare a separate highlighted list of all contracts requiring counterparty consent — not just notification. I need this by February 28.

Specifically, I want the following for each contract:

1. Contract name and counterparty
2. Annual contract value
3. Consent provision language (quoted verbatim from the agreement)
4. Any termination rights triggered by failure to obtain consent
5. Current relationship status — coordinate with Amanda Briggs's team for customer health data
6. Estimated timeline to obtain consent based on the counterparty's known procurement processes
7. Any historical precedent for consent requests with the same counterparty

For the leases, include the landlord name, remaining term, any recapture or early termination provisions, and the financial exposure if the landlord exercises any termination right.

This needs to be a clean, presentation-ready document that I can share with outside counsel. Use the standard privileged communication header and format it as an appendix to the main CoC summary.

One additional item — for the 14 executive employment agreements with CoC provisions, please prepare a separate analysis showing the aggregate cost of all triggered provisions (accelerated vesting, severance multipliers, COBRA continuation) assuming a hypothetical change of control occurring at various dates in Q2 2025. I need this for the financial modeling work that Priya's team is conducting. Coordinate with Jordan Michaels on the equity valuation assumptions.

Rachel Dominguez
General Counsel
Nextera Solutions

--- Reply ---
From: Nathan Foley
To: Rachel Dominguez
Date: February 20, 2025, 12:08 PM PST
Subject: RE: RE: Comprehensive Change-of-Control Summary — Draft Review

Rachel,

Understood on all points. I'll have the highlighted consent list ready by February 28 and will coordinate with Amanda Briggs's team on the customer health data. The executive CoC cost analysis will take a few additional days — I'll need compensation data from HR for the vesting calculations, and Jordan's team will need to provide the current 409A valuation and equity cap table. Targeting March 3 for that deliverable.

Quick question on the executive analysis: should I use the current 409A valuation for equity calculations, or do you want me to model at a range of valuations? The current 409A was set in October 2024 and may not reflect the company's recent performance trajectory.

I'll also flag that the two executive agreements with Section 280G gross-up provisions (Vasquez and Webb) will add meaningful cost if the transaction value triggers the excise tax threshold. I can model that separately if helpful.

Nathan

--- Reply ---
From: Rachel Dominguez
To: Nathan Foley
Date: February 20, 2025, 12:31 PM PST
Subject: RE: RE: RE: Comprehensive Change-of-Control Summary — Draft Review

Use a range: current 409A, 1.5x current, and 2x current. Three scenarios. Yes, model the 280G impact separately for Vasquez and Webb.

And make sure the consent list includes a recommendation on timing — how far in advance of any potential transaction would we need to begin the consent process, and what's the expected timeline for obtaining consents from each counterparty. I want to understand the critical path.

R.

--- End of Thread ---

OK team, great discussion. Key takeaways and action items:

2. Red accounts need immediate attention — Monday deep-dive at 10 AM

DOCUMENT 3 OF 4
TYPE: Internal Announcement
DATE: February 20, 2025
FROM: Vanessa Kline (VP People)
TO: All Employees
SUBJECT: February Hiring Update + New Office Space

Hello Nextera Team!

I'm excited to share our February people update with some great news about our continued growth and a few upcoming milestones that I hope everyone will participate in.

NEW TEAMMATES

We welcomed 14 new teammates in February! Our headcount is now 416 and growing. We've also secured additional office space on the 13th floor to accommodate our engineering team's growth.

Here's the breakdown of our February hires by department and role:

Engineering (8 new hires):
- 4 Backend Engineers — joining the core platform team, working on the data pipeline and recommendation engine infrastructure
- 2 ML/AI Engineers — joining the machine learning team, focused on model training and optimization for rec engine v4 planning
- 1 Platform Engineer — joining the infrastructure team, focused on Kubernetes operations and cloud cost optimization
- 1 QA Engineer — joining the quality assurance team, focused on automated testing for the API layer

Sales (3 new hires):
- 2 Account Executives — covering the Northeast and Southeast territories
- 1 Sales Development Representative — supporting the enterprise pipeline development team

Customer Success (2 new hires):
- 1 Customer Success Manager — managing mid-market accounts in the logistics vertical
- 1 Technical Account Manager — supporting our largest enterprise customers with implementation and integration projects

Legal (1 new hire):
- 1 Paralegal — supporting the expanded contract review and compliance workload in Rachel Dominguez's legal department

A warm welcome to every one of our new colleagues! If you haven't had a chance to introduce yourself yet, please take a moment to say hello — whether that's stopping by their desk, sending a Slack message, or joining one of the new hire lunch sessions that we organize every other Friday. Our culture of welcoming new teammates is something I hear about consistently in onboarding feedback, and I'd like to keep that reputation strong.

OFFICE EXPANSION — 13TH FLOOR

As many of you have noticed, our 14th floor headquarters has been getting a bit cozy, especially in the engineering wing where we've been doubling up on desks in some of the open-plan areas. I'm happy to announce that we've secured additional space on the 13th floor of our building. Here are the details:

- Location: 13th floor, same building as our 14th floor headquarters
- Space: 8,000 square feet
- Availability: March 1, 2025
- Purpose: Engineering team expansion workspace
- Capacity: Approximately 60 desks, 2 large meeting rooms (12-person each), 4 phone booths, and a small kitchen area with coffee/snack service
- Lease term: 18 months

The 13th floor will primarily house the platform engineering and ML/AI teams, with Marcus Webb's engineering leadership overseeing the space allocation. We chose to keep the engineering teams together rather than splitting across departments to maintain the collaboration dynamics that Marcus's team has built.

Facilities will begin the move-in setup starting next week, including furniture installation, IT infrastructure (networking, monitors, peripherals), and kitchen equipment. We expect the space to be fully operational by mid-March, with a formal "opening day" celebration planned for March 17 (St. Patrick's Day theme — why not?).

A few logistical notes about the 13th floor:
- Building access badges will work for the 13th floor starting March 1. No action needed on your part.
- The 13th floor elevator lobby will have the same visitor check-in process as the 14th floor.
- WiFi network name will be "Nextera-13" and will automatically connect if you're on the Nextera network profile.
- Parking and building amenities (gym, cafe, bike storage) remain unchanged.

The 18-month lease term is shorter than our usual 36-month terms, giving us flexibility as we evaluate our long-term space needs. Our 14th floor headquarters lease runs through 2029, so that remains our primary home base for the foreseeable future.

SPRING RECRUITING

Spring recruiting is in full swing. We're targeting 20+ hires in Q2 across engineering, sales, and customer success.

Current open positions: 32

If you know talented people who would be a great fit at Nextera, please use the employee referral program through Greenhouse. Referral bonuses remain at $5,000 for engineering roles and $3,000 for all other positions. Bonuses are paid 50% at hire date and 50% after the referred employee's 90-day mark.

Key positions we're actively hiring for:

Engineering:
- Senior Software Engineer, Platform (2 openings) — backend systems, distributed computing
- Machine Learning Engineer (2 openings) — model development, feature engineering
- Staff Engineer, Distributed Systems (1 opening) — architecture and technical leadership
- Engineering Manager, Data Pipeline (1 opening) — team leadership, pipeline optimization
- Security Engineer (1 opening) — application security, vulnerability management

Sales:
- Account Executive, Enterprise (3 openings) — $500K+ ACV target accounts
- Solutions Architect (2 openings) — technical pre-sales support

Customer Success:
- Customer Success Manager, Strategic Accounts (2 openings) — enterprise relationship management

Product:
- Product Manager, Integrations (1 opening) — partner ecosystem and API strategy

Full descriptions are posted on our careers page (nextera.io/careers) and on the internal job board in Greenhouse. Hiring managers for each role are listed in Greenhouse — feel free to reach out directly if you want to learn more about a specific team.

EMPLOYEE ENGAGEMENT SURVEY

Our annual employee engagement survey launches March 3. Your feedback matters — please take the 15 minutes to complete it.

Survey details:
- Launch date: Monday, March 3, 2025
- Close date: Monday, March 17, 2025 (two-week window)
- Platform: Culture Amp
- Estimated completion time: 15 minutes
- Anonymity: All responses are anonymous and reported in aggregate only. Results are shared at the team level only when a team has 5+ respondents to protect individual privacy.

Last year's engagement score was 78 out of 100, which placed us in the 72nd percentile among Culture Amp's technology company benchmark. Our goal this year is to maintain or improve that score while addressing the top feedback themes from last year's survey.

Last year's top three improvement areas were:
1. Career development opportunities — employees wanted clearer pathways for advancement and more structured mentorship programs
2. Cross-team collaboration — teams felt siloed and wanted more opportunities to work with colleagues from other departments
3. Meeting load — employees reported spending too much time in meetings and not enough in focused work

Here's what we've done in response to that feedback over the past 12 months:
- Launched the internal mobility program in September 2024 — 7 employees have transferred between departments since launch
- Created the cross-functional project initiative in November 2024 — 3 cross-team projects currently underway (Axion integration, rec engine v3 optimization, and the customer data platform project)
- Implemented "No Meeting Wednesdays" starting January 2025 — early feedback has been positive, with 89% of employees reporting they use the day for focused work
- Added 2 additional professional development days per year (now 5 total) — uptake has been strong, with 42% of employees already using at least one PD day in January

I'm genuinely curious to see if these changes have moved the needle. Please participate — the higher our response rate, the more meaningful the data becomes. Last year we achieved an 85% response rate, and I'd love to beat that this year.

UPCOMING HR DATES

Please add these to your calendars:
- February 28: Q1 performance review conversations due — managers, please ensure you've completed these with each direct report
- March 1: Updated severance policy effective (all employees were notified in January)
- March 3-17: Employee engagement survey open (Culture Amp)
- March 5: Retention Bonus Program final information session (eligible employees only, calendar invite sent separately)

Weekly Operating Metrics Snapshot

All key metrics continue to track ahead of plan for Q1 2025. New bookings are at 38% attainment with five weeks remaining in the quarter, consistent with historical pacing. Cash position remains strong at $24.2M with over 13 months of runway at current burn rate. Headcount reached 416 following 14 February hires.

REVENUE AND BOOKINGS

Bookings Analysis:
- Q1 bookings pace: $14.1M of $36.8M quota at day 48 of the quarter (38% attainment)
- Historical Q1 pacing: approximately 55-60% of bookings close in March, the final month of the quarter

The $145K churn this month is the Pacific Freight account, which moved to a competitor on pricing. No other accounts are in active churn risk based on current contract terms and health scores, though the customer success team has flagged 15 accounts in red health status for ongoing monitoring.

| Monthly Burn Rate | $1.8M | $1.7M | +$0.1M |
| Runway (months) | 13.4 | 15.2 | (1.8) |

The $1.6M decrease in cash is attributable to three primary factors:
1. 13th floor lease security deposit and initial fit-out costs: $480K
2. Incremental February payroll for 14 new hires (prorated): $320K

Cash position remains strong. The vendor payment timing will normalize in March as these are quarterly/annual items. The 13th floor costs are one-time. At the current burn rate of $1.8M per month, the company has approximately 13.4 months of runway, which is above the board-mandated minimum of 12 months.
